annotations.text: One syndrome that deserves mention in this case is arrhythmogenic right ventricular dysplasia, which is primarily a right ventricular cardiomyopathy, frequently with little or no left ventricular involvement.
ustained monomorphic ventricular tachycardia is often associated with a poor outcome in patients with heart disease.
The presence of sustained monomorphic ventricular tachycardia is often associated with a poor outcome in patients with heart disease.
In view of the presence of thoracic lymphadenopathy in this young nonsmoker, the possibility of lymphoma should be considered.
The coxsackie group B viruses are most commonly implicated.
Although tuberculous myocarditis is a possibility in this case, several findings provide evidence against this diagnosis.
Generalized anergy is more consistent with lymphoma or sarcoidosis.
The diagnosis of sarcoidosis should be strongly considered in any young patient with conduction-system disease and ventricular tachycardia.
The pattern of lung involvement, although atypical, is consistent with pulmonary sarcoidosis.
the cardiomyopathy, conduction-system dysfunction, and sustained ventricular tachycardia are common manifestations of cardiac sarcoidosis.
Since previous myocardial biopsies had been nondiagnostic, a repeated myocardial biopsy, even in the light of the progressive left ventricular dysfunction, would not have been my first choice.

Extensive myocardial involvement can also occur with few extracardiac symptoms and may be correlated with sudden cardiac death, although this point is disputed.
